title: One-vs-Rest (OVR) Multiclass
concept: One-vs-Rest (OVR) solves multi-class classification by training one binary (yes/no) model for each class, comparing it against all others combined.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Under what condition is a non-linear decision boundary preferred over a linear one?

options.text: When the classes are nested or mixed in shapes (like concentric circles) that a straight line cannot separate
When the dataset is very small and we want to avoid overfitting
When we want the model to train faster and use less memory

exercises.explain: Correct! When data is nested or complex, a straight line (linear) fails, making a curved or non-linear boundary necessary. Now, what if we have more than two classes?

cards.title: Fruit sorting
cards.body: If you have a bin of apples, bananas, and cherries, how does a simple robot sort them? It can ask three questions: 'Is it an apple or not?', 'Is it a banana or not?', and 'Is it a cherry or not?'

cards.title: One-vs-Rest, defined
cards.body: One-vs-Rest (OVR), or One-vs-All, is a method for multi-class classification that trains one binary classifier per class. Each classifier predicts 'this class' vs 'everything else'.

cards.title: Job interview
cards.body: Think of a job interview with three candidates. Instead of comparing them to each other, the boss rates each candidate individually: 'Hire or don't hire?'. The candidate with the highest rating wins the job.

cards.title: Cat vs everything else
cards.body: For classifying images into [Cat, Dog, Bird], OVR trains 3 separate models: 1) Cat vs [Dog/Bird], 2) Dog vs [Cat/Bird], 3) Bird vs [Cat/Dog]. The image goes to the model that outputs the highest probability.

cards.title: Myth: OVR needs new algorithms
cards.body: False. OVR is just a wrapper! It lets you reuse simple binary tools like logistic regression to solve multi-class problems without changing the core math.

cards.title: OVR classification layout
cards.body: How a multi-class problem is broken down into simple yes/no questions:

exercises.prompt: If you have 5 different classes to predict, how many binary classifiers will One-vs-Rest (OVR) train?
exercises.explain: OVR trains exactly one binary classifier per class. For 5 classes, it trains 5 classifiers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In OVR, the final prediction is decided by whichever binary classifier outputs the highest probability score.
exercises.explain: True! The class whose corresponding model outputs the highest probability score is selected as the final prediction.

exercises.sentence: A One-vs-Rest model classifying vehicles into Car, Truck, or Bike will train a classifier specifically comparing Car vs ___.
exercises.explain: OVR compares the target class (Car) against all the rest combined (Truck and Bike).

exercises.prompt: Which system is using a One-vs-Rest (OVR) strategy?

options.text: An app that trains separate models for [Cat vs Not-Cat], [Dog vs Not-Dog], and [Bird vs Not-Bird]
An app that trains models for [Cat vs Dog], [Dog vs Bird], and [Cat vs Bird]
An app that predicts a single continuous score from 1 to 10

exercises.explain: Training each class against 'Not-that-class' is the hallmark of the One-vs-Rest approach.

exercises.prompt: What is a potential downside of the OVR approach when training on unbalanced data?

options.text: Each classifier's 'Rest' group becomes much larger than its 'One' group
It requires calculating millions of complex derivative equations
It only works if you have exactly three classes
It can only be used with curved decision boundaries

exercises.explain: Yes! When comparing one class to all others, the 'Rest' group (all other classes combined) can dwarf the single class, causing class imbalance.

exercises.prompt: You have a model predicting weather: [Sunny, Rainy, Snowy]. The Sunny model outputs 0.85, the Rainy model outputs 0.15, and the Snowy model outputs 0.05. How does OVR classify this?

options.text: Sunny, because its classifier produced the highest probability score
Rainy, because it's a safer default option
Uncertain, because the scores do not add up to exactly 1.0
Snowy, because it is the most extreme weather condition

exercises.explain: Excellent! In OVR, the classifier with the highest output score wins the prediction. Even if the scores don't sum to 1.0, 0.85 is the clear winner. You've mastered One-vs-Rest!
